frameworks.name: Delivery-Change Moral High Ground Pitch
frameworks.definition: A three-part reframe for announcing a delivery reduction: (1) acknowledge you have been falling short of a stated promise, (2) position the change as restoring integrity rather than cutting service, (3) list reasons the new format benefits clients, making it socially costly for any client to object.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained rather than demand-constrained, changing the selling model is the wrong lever — the delivery model is the correct constraint to address first.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business with a single delivery person or limited delivery capacity
Organic demand already exists and the founder cannot take more customers
claims.mechanism: Modifying the demand side (e.g., switching to evergreen enrollment) increases inbound pressure on a system that is already at capacity, worsening the constraint rather than solving it.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business is demand-constrained (no inbound leads)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a founder who responds rapidly to every client inquiry trains clients to make more frequent requests, because quick response is experienced as a reward that reinforces the reaching-out behavior.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is the primary point of contact
claims.mechanism: Variable-ratio reinforcement: clients learn that reaching out produces fast access to the founder, so they repeat the behavior more frequently; the founder has effectively trained the client to demand more time.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in the early underdog stage, a founder's personal accessibility (giving clients a cell-phone number) is a genuine competitive differentiator, but once credibility is proven, that same accessibility becomes a scaling liability that must be removed.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Early stage: founder has no established reputation and is competing against larger providers
Later stage: founder has a track record and a cohort of satisfied clients
claims.mechanism: The value proposition shifts from 'you get me personally because no one else will give you access' to 'I have proven enough that you do not need direct access to get results'; the narrative flip is enabled by demonstrated credibility.
bounded_by: Does not apply to businesses where the founder's personal touch is the core product and cannot be systematized

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a service founder who sets client expectations higher than the contractual promise is creating the bottleneck themselves, because the over-delivery becomes the de facto standard clients defend.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is delivering significantly more than what was promised in the original sale
claims.mechanism: When delivery exceeds the promise, clients anchor their expectations to the observed delivery level, not the contracted level; any future reduction feels like a breach even if it is technically within scope.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when reducing delivery volume, the founder must reframe the change as fulfilling an unkept promise rather than cutting service, positioning it as a moral obligation to improve, not a cost-saving measure.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is reducing the touchpoints or hours delivered to existing clients
Clients have come to expect the higher delivery level
claims.mechanism: Framing the reduction as restoring integrity to a previously broken promise makes it socially and emotionally costly for clients to object, because objecting implies they are questioning the founder's integrity.
bounded_by: Requires that the founder can identify a genuine promise they have been failing to keep; purely fabricated moral framing will be perceived as manipulation

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when transitioning a delivery model, a founder will lose some existing clients and should accept this loss, because the departing clients make room for new clients who pay more and carry none of the legacy expectations.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business making a structural delivery change
Existing cohort was acquired under older, higher-touch terms
claims.mechanism: Clients who churn during a delivery restructure were anchored to legacy expectations that are incompatible with the new model; their departure clears pricing and expectation anchors, allowing the founder to reset at a higher price point with new clients.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a founder who is the sole delivery person in a coaching business is directly capping the revenue ceiling of that business at their personal delivery capacity, and that either hiring a second delivery person or restructuring the delivery model is the mandatory unlock.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is the only coach or delivery person
Business has demonstrated organic demand but cannot grow revenue
claims.mechanism: Revenue is a direct product of clients served multiplied by price; if the founder's time is the constraint on clients served, revenue cannot exceed the founder's capacity unless delivery is restructured or delegated.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a founder choosing to continue an unsustainable delivery model because of fear of client reaction is out of integrity with their stated mission to help more clients, and that acknowledging this misalignment can provide the emotional permission to change the model.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder has an explicit mission to serve a larger number of clients
Current delivery model makes serving more clients structurally impossible
claims.mechanism: Framing the status quo as a breach of the founder's own stated mission converts the change from a sacrifice into a moral imperative, removing the psychological resistance to restructuring.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that founders who are supply-constrained should not modify their demand-generation model (e.g., switching from cohort to evergreen enrollment) until the delivery constraint is resolved, because expanding demand into a full-capacity system creates a promise-delivery gap.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business is at or near full delivery capacity
Founder is considering changing offer structure or enrollment windows to grow revenue
claims.mechanism: Opening enrollment while at capacity fills the pipeline with clients the business cannot serve, creating churn downstream when the service fails to meet expectations set during the sale.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the delivery constraint can be instantly relieved by hiring or outsourcing

examples.text: The founder of Dogco Launch (a pet care coaching and consulting business modeled after Gym Launch) was doing 90-minute weekly coaching sessions with six active cohorts, plus daily office hours, totaling roughly 45 hours per week on Zoom. He wanted to grow from $356k to $3M over two years but could not take new clients because he was the only coach. He had created an expectation problem by over-delivering on access and responsiveness, which he had initially used as a differentiator to establish goodwill in a new industry. The result was that clients expected founder-level access, making it operationally and emotionally difficult to reduce delivery without risk of churn.

Alex Hormozi described a delivery-change communication approach he has personally used: rather than announcing a reduction in what clients receive, he would say he had listened to client feedback, identified a way the current model was failing to honor a commitment he made, and was changing the format for 17 specific reasons that benefited clients. He would then note that staying in integrity with his original promise required the change. This framing makes it socially untenable for a client to object without appearing to question the founder's integrity.

practices.text: When clients request unscheduled founder time, route them to a documented resource or to the next available calendar slot rather than responding immediately; this retrains client behavior away from ad-hoc requests.

When announcing a delivery model change to existing clients, lead with new information that has come to light, state the decision the new information requires, explain the actions that follow, and list multiple specific benefits to the client.
Before changing the selling model of a supply-constrained business, first resolve the delivery constraint (hire, delegate, restructure sessions) to ensure supply can meet the new demand level.
